note: Verbatim phrases of the locked WC-PB-11 narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence pauses (never rewritten, summarized, or reordered; spellings kept exactly, e.g. 'read only', 'four oh four', 'IT and Support'). Block windows are MEASURED onsets, not the nominal formula: Sigma(ffprobe per-file audio + 0.4) puts the s09 end at 146.829s, which overshoots the ffprobe-probed master (146.521s) by 0.308s (> 0.3 tolerance) because the concatenated MP3 was joined with gaps slightly under 0.4s (drift accumulates to -0.300s by s09). Onsets were therefore measured by normalized cross-correlation of each WC-PB-11-sNN.mp3 (0.75s template) against the joined master at 8 kHz mono (all 9 blocks matched at NCC 0.9999-1.000). Master ffprobe duration 146.521s (PCM-decoded 146.480s).

phrases.text: A caller wants two things: who changed it, and what it said before.
Two pages answer that between them, and you read them in order.
One rule before you start.
The audit trail is read only.
It's only worth anything because nobody corrects it.
Open IT and Support, then Audit.
You land on the Audit Log, and the sidebar lists three pages.
You need two of them today: the Audit Log for who did what,
and Change History for the before and after.
User Activity is login history.
That one belongs to a different playbook.
Read the log.
Seven columns tell you who touched the record, when,
from which location, and from which IP address.
The change you're chasing is an Updated row.
Settings changes land here too, with the module reading Settings.
Don't scroll a busy log.
Filter it, or search it.
Now the page the caller actually wants.
In the sidebar, click Change History.
One quirk from today's capture: typing its address directly returned a four oh four.
So you reach it this way, through the audit sidebar, not by URL.
Narrow it to the record.
Filter down to the module in question so one record's changes stand alone.
You're reading a history, not editing one.
And here's the answer.
Each change is recorded at field level: the field, its old value, its new value,
with the user and the timestamp.
That row is, literally, what was it before.
Now put the two pages together.
Change History gives you the field and the exact old and new values.
The Audit Log gives you the user, the date and time, and the IP address it came from.
The price changed is not an answer.
The two values are.
Write the packet.
The record, the field, both values, the user, the timestamp, the IP address, and screenshots.
You never reverse the change from here.
A correction happens in the record's own module, or gets escalated.
And if the change looks unauthorized, that's a security escalation,
and the next tier wants the IP and the user first.
That's the order.
Audit Log first, for who, when, where, and which IP.
Change History second, for the field-level old to new.
Then the exact answer, or a clean escalation.
Nothing edited, nothing touched.
